defaultDescription: A practical starter stack selected from your quiz answers and the currently published tool catalog.
defaultCostNotes: Start on free tiers where possible, then upgrade the tools attached to revenue, retention, or release confidence first.

recommendations.reason: A portable Postgres-backed backend covers auth, database, storage, and APIs without building infrastructure first.
recommendations.costNote: Free tier is usually enough for MVP validation; usage-based costs become clearer after launch.

recommendations.reason: Privacy-friendly product analytics keep the first stack lightweight while still showing activation and retention signals.
recommendations.costNote: Start with the free or indie-friendly plan; upgrade when funnels and cohorts become weekly operating metrics.

recommendations.reason: RevenueCat is the safest default for subscriptions because it handles entitlements, receipt validation, and revenue reporting across app stores.
recommendations.costNote: Good fit for free-to-start or under-$50 MVP budgets because the paid plan can wait until revenue exists.

recommendations.reason: A monetization stack still needs clean activation and retention events before paywall tests are meaningful.
recommendations.costNote: Keep analytics lean until paywall traffic is large enough for experiments.

recommendations.reason: Adapty becomes more attractive once the app is live because paywall experiments and subscription analytics can compound quickly.
recommendations.costNote: Worth considering when subscription optimization is already part of the weekly growth loop.

recommendations.reason: Supabase gives an indie team a real database, auth, storage, and server functions while keeping data portable.
recommendations.costNote: Free tier works well for prototypes and MVPs; paid usage scales with database and edge function needs.

recommendations.reason: Firebase remains a strong cross-platform default when realtime sync, auth, and hosted app infrastructure matter more than SQL portability.
recommendations.costNote: Free tier is useful, but watch usage-based costs before scale.

recommendations.reason: PostHog covers product analytics, funnels, feature flags, and experiments in one stack for teams ready to learn from behavior.
recommendations.costNote: Use the generous free tier first; tighten event volume before growth traffic arrives.

recommendations.reason: Amplitude is a better fit once product analytics becomes a shared decision system across acquisition, activation, and retention.
recommendations.costNote: Best when the app has enough usage to justify a more sophisticated analytics workspace.

recommendations.reason: Sentry gives clear crash and error visibility before release issues turn into support and review problems.
recommendations.costNote: Start on the free tier, then upgrade when alerting and release health become operationally important.

recommendations.reason: fastlane removes repetitive screenshot, metadata, and release tasks without adding another paid SaaS subscription.
recommendations.costNote: Open-source tooling keeps the monthly bill low; hosted CI may add cost later.

recommendations.reason: Appfigures is a practical ASO and competitor-tracking starting point for launch research and early store visibility.
recommendations.costNote: Use focused research windows before committing to a higher monthly ASO plan.

recommendations.reason: Framer is quick for polished app landing pages, waitlist messaging, and launch assets without a full CMS build.
recommendations.costNote: A lightweight landing page plan is usually enough before serious paid acquisition.

recommendations.reason: beehiiv keeps launch subscribers and product updates in one simple channel while the app is still finding demand.
recommendations.costNote: Begin with the free tier and pay only when the audience becomes a growth asset.

recommendations.reason: fastlane is open source and helps automate release chores without adding SaaS spend.
recommendations.costNote: Monthly software cost can stay at $0 if you run automations locally or on free CI minutes.

recommendations.reason: Appwrite is a good open-source-friendly backend option when keeping spend near zero matters most.
recommendations.costNote: Self-hosting can reduce SaaS bills but adds maintenance time.

recommendations.reason: For an iOS MVP, RevenueCat avoids custom StoreKit receipt and entitlement work.
recommendations.costNote: Keep subscription infrastructure free-to-start until revenue validates the app.

recommendations.reason: TelemetryDeck is a lean analytics default for iOS teams that want signal without a heavy setup.
recommendations.costNote: A sensible fit for a sub-$50 software stack.

recommendations.reason: OneSignal is useful once lifecycle messaging can bring real users back into the app.
recommendations.costNote: Delay paid messaging complexity until retention channels are worth optimizing.

recommendations.reason: Braze makes sense when lifecycle messaging, segmentation, and campaign orchestration justify a larger operating budget.
recommendations.costNote: Only consider this once retention and lifecycle campaigns are a real growth lever.

recommendations.reason: Codemagic is a strong hosted CI/CD fit when release reliability is worth more than minimizing monthly cost.
recommendations.costNote: Hosted build minutes are easier to justify once releases are frequent.
